content.text: Platform as a Service (PaaS) removes the operating system, runtime, middleware, and platform infrastructure from the customer's responsibility, leaving only the application code and data to manage. The provider handles patching, scaling, high availability, and runtime updates — letting development teams ship features instead of managing servers.

PaaS sits between IaaS (raw virtual machines) and SaaS (fully managed applications) in the cloud service model spectrum. The provider manages the full software stack below the application layer — hardware, virtualization, OS, web server, application runtime, and database engine. The customer supplies only the application code and its configuration. On Azure, PaaS encompasses a broad range of services:

Azure App Service is the flagship PaaS compute offering. It supports Python, Node.js, .NET, Java, PHP, and Ruby runtimes on both Linux and Windows. The service maintains the OS and runtime versions, applies security patches automatically, handles TLS certificate management, and provides built-in autoscaling. A deployment is as simple as pushing code via Git, GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps, or a ZIP upload — no server configuration required.

PaaS dramatically reduces the operational surface area of a system. With IaaS, each VM requires its own OS patching schedule, security hardening runbook, disk management procedures, and monitoring configuration. A ten-VM application might require a dedicated sysadmin. The equivalent PaaS deployment needs none of this — runtime updates happen automatically within a maintenance window, health checks restart failed instances without paging anyone, and scaling rules fire without human intervention.

For engineering teams, PaaS changes the economics of reliability. High availability that would require careful Availability Zone placement, load balancer health probe configuration, and VM startup script management on IaaS is built into App Service by default. Every App Service Standard tier and above plan runs on multiple instances across fault domains. The team writes a web application; the platform provides the reliability primitives.
— operating system patches, kernel upgrades, and security hardening are handled by Azure; there are no CVEs to track at the OS layer
— App Service Standard and above provide 99.95% SLA out of the box; no explicit AZ configuration required for basic HA
— swap staging and production environments atomically with zero-downtime blue-green deployments; swap back instantly if a release causes issues
— scale out to additional instances on CPU, memory, HTTP queue depth, or a custom metric; scale in during off-peak hours to control costs
— App Service provisions and auto-renews Let's Encrypt certificates for custom domains; no certbot cron jobs needed
— assign a managed identity to an App Service and it can authenticate to Key Vault, SQL Database, Blob Storage, and Service Bus without any stored credentials
— no infrastructure to provision before testing; push code and the platform handles the rest; deployment times are seconds to minutes versus VM provisioning at 1–3 minutes per instance
— only supported runtimes can run on App Service; exotic languages, custom kernel modules, or applications requiring root access cannot be hosted on PaaS
— new language or framework versions sometimes arrive weeks after release on App Service; applications that need the latest patch immediately cannot always upgrade
— App Service instances share a network-mounted home directory; local file writes are discouraged and lost on scale-out events; use Blob Storage or Azure Files for persistence

— App Service Plans bill per hour regardless of traffic; a B1 plan running 24/7 with zero requests still accrues its full monthly cost; for truly bursty workloads, Azure Functions consumption plan may be more economical
— infrastructure-level issues (host failures, network fabric problems) are opaque; troubleshooting is limited to application logs and platform metrics rather than full OS visibility
Every App Service application runs on an

— a pool of virtual machine resources with a defined SKU. The plan determines the compute size, number of instances, and features available. Multiple apps can share one plan at no additional cost, making plans the unit of billing rather than individual apps. Plans come in several tiers:
— development only; shared infrastructure, CPU quotas, no custom domains, no SLA
— dedicated VMs, custom domains, manual scaling only; suitable for dev/test with light production use
— autoscaling, 5 deployment slots, daily backups, 99.95% SLA; the minimum for production workloads
— VNet integration, private endpoints, 20 deployment slots, enhanced compute; required for production apps needing private networking
— App Service Environment (ASE): dedicated infrastructure in your VNet; for regulated industries requiring fully private compute
Deployment slots are live App Service instances with separate URLs. The
slot serves real traffic. Additional slots (e.g.,
) receive new deployments first. After validation, a

operation atomically routes production traffic to the staging slot and vice versa. The old production code moves to staging, enabling instant rollback by swapping again. Slot settings (e.g., connection strings flagged as "slot-sticky") remain bound to their slot during swaps, so the new code automatically gets production credentials.

Managed databases extend PaaS to the data layer. Azure SQL Database provides a fully managed SQL Server engine: automated backups with point-in-time restore up to 35 days, zone-redundant high availability with automatic failover, read replicas, and built-in query performance advisor. There is no SQL Server instance to configure, no transaction log backups to schedule, and no failover cluster to maintain. Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server similarly manages the Postgres engine with support for extensions, logical replication, and configurable maintenance windows.

The diagram below shows a production PaaS web application on Azure. Azure Front Door handles global load balancing and WAF at the edge. App Service runs the application code and authenticates to downstream services via a managed identity — no stored connection string passwords. Key Vault holds any configuration secrets. Azure Monitor collects logs and metrics from all tiers.

attrs.alt: PaaS architecture on Azure showing Azure Front Door, App Service with managed identity, Azure SQL Database, Redis Cache, Blob Storage, Key Vault, and Azure Monitor

content.text: The managed identity pattern shown in the diagram is critical: App Service receives a system-assigned identity when the feature is enabled. Azure RBAC grants this identity the appropriate roles on each downstream service (e.g.,
on the storage account). The application code authenticates using
, which automatically acquires a token from the instance metadata service. No password, connection string secret, or API key is needed in the environment.
PaaS is the right choice for the majority of new web applications, APIs, and background processing workloads that use standard runtimes. Ideal scenarios:
— any Python/FastAPI, Node.js/Express, ASP.NET Core, or Spring Boot application maps directly to App Service
— choose Azure SQL Database or Azure Database for PostgreSQL over running Postgres on a VM unless specific extensions or configurations are unavailable
— PaaS eliminates the need for OS-level expertise, making it ideal for product teams deploying and operating their own services
— deployment slots make zero-downtime release and instant rollback trivially easy, enabling continuous delivery pipelines

PaaS is a poor fit when the application requires a specific OS, custom kernel module, specific database engine not available as a managed service, or GPU access. In those cases, IaaS (VM or AKS node pools) provides the required control.
Deploy a Python web app to App Service

# Create a resource group and App Service Plan
az group create --name paas-demo-rg --location eastus

az appservice plan create \
  --name paas-plan \
  --resource-group paas-demo-rg \
  --sku P1V3 \
  --is-linux

# Create the web app (name must be globally unique)
az webapp create \
  --resource-group paas-demo-rg \
  --plan paas-plan \
  --name myapp-$(openssl rand -hex 4) \
  --runtime "PYTHON|3.11"

# Enable system-assigned managed identity
az webapp identity assign \
  --resource-group paas-demo-rg \
  --name myapp

# Set environment variables (non-secret config)
az webapp config appsettings set \
  --resource-group paas-demo-rg \
  --name myapp \
  --settings \
    ENVIRONMENT=production \
    LOG_LEVEL=INFO \
    DATABASE_HOST=mydb.database.windows.net

# Deploy code from local directory using ZIP deploy
zip -r app.zip . --exclude '*.pyc' --exclude '__pycache__/*'
az webapp deploy \
  --resource-group paas-demo-rg \
  --name myapp \
  --src-path app.zip \
  --type zip
Create and connect a managed PostgreSQL database

# Create Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
az postgres flexible-server create \
  --resource-group paas-demo-rg \
  --name myapp-db \
  --location eastus \
  --admin-user dbadmin \
  --admin-password "$(openssl rand -base64 24)" \
  --sku-name Standard_B1ms \
  --tier Burstable \
  --version 16 \
  --storage-size 32 \
  --backup-retention 7

# Allow the App Service outbound IPs to reach the DB
# (Better: use VNet integration + private endpoint in production)
APP_IPS=$(az webapp show \
  --resource-group paas-demo-rg \
  --name myapp \
  --query outboundIpAddresses --output tsv)
for IP in $(echo $APP_IPS | tr ',' ' '); do
  az postgres flexible-server firewall-rule create \
    --resource-group paas-demo-rg \
    --name myapp-db \
    --rule-name "allow-app-$IP" \
    --start-ip-address "$IP" \
    --end-ip-address "$IP"
done

# Store only the hostname in app settings (password goes to Key Vault)
az webapp config appsettings set \
  --resource-group paas-demo-rg \
  --name myapp \
  --settings DATABASE_URL="postgresql://dbadmin@myapp-db.postgres.database.azure.com/appdb?sslmode=require"
Configure a deployment slot and swap

# Create a staging slot (requires Standard tier or above)
az webapp deployment slot create \
  --resource-group paas-demo-rg \
  --name myapp \
  --slot staging \
  --configuration-source myapp

# Deploy new version to staging, not production
az webapp deploy \
  --resource-group paas-demo-rg \
  --name myapp \
  --slot staging \
  --src-path app-v2.zip \
  --type zip

# Verify staging is healthy at: https://myapp-staging.azurewebsites.net
# Then swap staging -> production (zero downtime)
az webapp deployment slot swap \
  --resource-group paas-demo-rg \
  --name myapp \
  --slot staging \
  --target-slot production

# If the new version has a bug, swap back immediately
# az webapp deployment slot swap --slot production --target-slot staging
Python FastAPI app reading config from Key Vault via managed identity
GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline for App Service

name: Deploy to Azure App Service
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'

      - name: Install and test
        run: |
          pip install -r requirements.txt
          pytest tests/ -q

      - name: Deploy to staging slot
        uses: azure/webapps-deploy@v3
        with:
          app-name: myapp
          slot-name: staging
          publish-profile: ${{ secrets.AZURE_WEBAPP_PUBLISH_PROFILE_STAGING }}
          package: .

      - name: Swap staging to production
        uses: azure/CLI@v2
        with:
          azcliversion: latest
          inlineScript: |
            az webapp deployment slot swap \
              --resource-group paas-demo-rg \
              --name myapp \
              --slot staging \
              --target-slot production

A team of six engineers builds a B2C e-commerce platform on Azure PaaS. The storefront is a React SPA served from Azure Static Web Apps (free tier). The order API runs on App Service Premium P2v3, autoscaling from 2 to 8 instances based on HTTP queue depth. Azure SQL Database Business Critical hosts the product and order data with zone-redundant HA and automatic failover. Azure Cache for Redis Standard tier caches product catalog queries and session tokens. Azure Service Bus Premium handles order events between the API and fulfillment workers running as Azure Functions. Azure Front Door accelerates global delivery with CDN caching of product images stored in Blob Storage. The entire infrastructure is defined in Bicep and deployed via GitHub Actions. No VM is ever patched. The team ships three production releases per day. Monthly infrastructure cost at 50,000 daily active users: approximately $1,800.
— embedding database passwords in app settings or source code; use Key Vault references in App Service settings (
) so secrets are never exposed in the portal or logs
— Basic (B1/B2/B3) plans lack autoscaling, deployment slots, and have no SLA; always use Standard or Premium for production workloads
— without a configured health check path, App Service cannot detect and replace unhealthy instances; configure a
endpoint and enable App Service health monitoring
— App Service instances may restart and lose local state; local writes are not shared across scale-out instances; use Blob Storage for files and an external cache for session state
— bypassing staging slots removes the ability to validate a release before it reaches users; always deploy to a staging slot and swap
— App Service unloads apps after 20 minutes of inactivity by default (except on Free/Shared); enable Always On in Basic and above to prevent cold-start latency on the first request after idle
Use Key Vault references for all secrets in app settings — Azure resolves them at runtime so the secret value never appears in the App Service configuration UI or deployment history
Enable VNet integration (Premium tier) and configure private endpoints for databases, Redis, and Service Bus — eliminates database connections traversing the public internet
Configure autoscale with a minimum of 2 instances for production — a single instance cannot handle traffic during deployments or rolling updates
Enable Application Insights to capture request traces, exceptions, dependencies, and custom metrics without any SDK changes (via auto-instrumentation for supported runtimes)
Pin the runtime stack version in app settings to avoid unexpected upgrades during platform maintenance; test runtime upgrades explicitly in staging before allowing them in production

App Service scales horizontally by adding instances to the plan. Autoscale rules fire within 5 minutes of the trigger condition being met, and new instances warm up by running the application startup sequence before receiving traffic — a process that typically takes 30–90 seconds for a Python/Node.js app. To reduce scale-out latency for latency-sensitive workloads, pre-warm instances using App Service's warm-up feature, which boots additional instances when scale-out is anticipated based on current traffic trends.

Database performance on PaaS is governed by the managed service SKU. Azure SQL Database DTU-based tiers bundle compute and I/O; the vCore model allows independent scaling of CPU and storage with predictable resource limits. For read-heavy workloads, add read replicas to Azure Database for PostgreSQL and route read queries to the replica connection string. Azure Cache for Redis reduces database load for frequently read, rarely updated data — catalog queries, user preferences, and computed aggregates are ideal cache targets.
Azure App Service VS Code Extension
— deploy, stream logs, and manage app settings directly from the editor; useful for quick iterations during development
— official action for deploying to App Service from GitHub workflows; supports slot targeting and publish profile authentication
— first-class Azure integration with built-in App Service deployment tasks, environment gates, and release pipelines
— APM tool deeply integrated with App Service; auto-instrumentation captures incoming requests, dependencies, exceptions, and custom telemetry with near-zero code changes
— diagnostic console built into every App Service; provides file browser, log streaming, bash/PowerShell shell, and process explorer for debugging deployed applications

PaaS shifts much of the security burden to the provider but does not eliminate it. The provider secures the OS and runtime against known CVEs. The customer remains responsible for application-layer security, identity configuration, network access controls, and secret management.

— built-in identity provider integration (Entra ID, Google, GitHub, Apple) that authenticates requests before they reach application code; enable for internal tools and B2C apps without writing auth code
— limit inbound traffic to specific IP ranges or service tags (e.g., allow only Azure Front Door); ensures the app is not directly reachable if Front Door is the intended entry point
Managed TLS and minimum TLS version
— enforce TLS 1.2 minimum in App Service TLS settings; platform manages certificate renewal automatically for both App Service managed certificates and custom certificates
Azure SQL Database transparent data encryption
— enabled by default; all data at rest is encrypted with AES-256 using a service-managed key; bring-your-own-key (BYOK) is available for higher compliance requirements
— Azure SQL Database and Azure Database for PostgreSQL perform automated backups every 5–12 minutes with geo-redundant storage; point-in-time restore is available up to 35 days

PaaS is like renting a fully equipped commercial kitchen versus building your own. With IaaS, the kitchen is empty — you bring your own appliances, pots, knives, and cleaning supplies, and you are responsible for maintaining and replacing them. With PaaS, the kitchen is equipped and maintained by the venue: refrigeration, ovens, dishwashers, and ventilation are managed for you. You bring your recipes (application code) and ingredients (data) and focus entirely on cooking. The venue handles equipment maintenance, safety inspections, and cleaning between sessions. The constraint is that you cook within the kitchen's layout and the appliances provided — you cannot install a custom kiln. That constraint is the price of not managing the kitchen.
PaaS eliminates OS management, runtime patching, and server provisioning — the team writes application code and the platform handles the infrastructure below it
App Service Plans are the billing unit; apps hosted on the same plan share capacity and cost at no additional charge
Deployment slots (Standard tier and above) enable zero-downtime blue-green releases and instant rollback by swapping staging and production environments
Use managed identity for all service-to-service authentication — never store database passwords or API keys in app settings
Managed databases (Azure SQL Database, Azure Database for PostgreSQL) extend PaaS to the data tier — automated backups, HA failover, and query optimization without DBA intervention
PaaS imposes runtime constraints; choose IaaS or containers when the application requires a custom OS, specific kernel modules, or a runtime not supported on App Service
